coredata.dc:title: L-Tryptophan functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescence indicator for pH detection in real water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoxiong
Zhang, Wenjun
Tang, Shaokang
coredata.dc:description: L-Tryptophan (Trp) functionalized graphene quantum dots (Trp-GQDs) were prepared via a thermal cracking method, using Trp and citric acid (CA) as raw material. The as-obtained Trp-GQDs show stronger blue photoluminescence (PL) than simple graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The presence of Trp can obviously improve the luminescent properties of GQDs. The possible mechanism was investigated for the formation and luminescence properties of the Trp-GQDs. Furthermore, the Trp-GQDs exhibit a good liner relationship between fluorescence response and pH variation within the pH range of 4–12. More importantly, Trp-GQDs still perform well in sensitivity to pH values in real water samples. The results indicate that the probe shows great potential in pH detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tryptophan
Graphene quantum dots
Luminescent
pH sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detail and description of experiment
Morphology and structural characterization of Trp-GQDs
PL response of Trp-GQDs for pH
Zeta potential analysis of Trp-GQDs at different pH
pH detection of environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of synthesis of Trp-GQDs.
(A) The size distributions of Trp-GQDs. (B) HRTEM image and (C) XRD spectra of the as-prepared Trp-GQDs.
FT-IR spectrum of CA (a), Trp (b) and Trp-GQDs (c).
XPS (A), C 1 s (B), N 1 s (C) and O 1 s (D) spectra of the as-prepared Trp-GQDs.

The optical properties of the as-prepared Trp-GQDs. (A) UV spectra of the Trp-GQDs (a), Trp (b) and CA (c). Inset: photographs taken under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (B) Fluorescence spectra of the Trp-GQDs and GQDs.
(A) Fluorescence intensity varied with BR buffer of different pH (4–12). (B) Standard curve line.
The linear relationship between pH and zeta potential.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of Trp-GQDs at the two pH 4 and 12 for cycle experiments；(B) The reversible fluorescence response curve over seven consecutive pH cycles between 4 and 12.
Water sample pH results measured in two ways.

head.ce:title: L-Tryptophan functionalized graphene quantum dots as a fluorescence indicator for pH detection in real water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: L-Tryptophan (Trp) functionalized graphene quantum dots (Trp-GQDs) were prepared via a thermal cracking method, using Trp and citric acid (CA) as raw material. The as-obtained Trp-GQDs show stronger blue photoluminescence (PL) than simple graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The presence of Trp can obviously improve the luminescent properties of GQDs. The possible mechanism was investigated for the formation and luminescence properties of the Trp-GQDs. Furthermore, the Trp-GQDs exhibit a good liner relationship between fluorescence response and pH variation within the pH range of 4–12. More importantly, Trp-GQDs still perform well in sensitivity to pH values in real water samples. The results indicate that the probe shows great potential in pH detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, the source of life, is an indispensable substance in human life. The quality of water directly affects the human health. As an important parameter of water quality, pH value can directly reflect the quality of water. As the saying goes, “Illness finds its way in by the mouth.” According to reports, many diseases including cancers, shock, Alzheimer’s disease and rheumatoid arthritis have already been linked to abnormal intracellular pH values [1–6]. Controlling the pH value of drinking water is conducive to the rehabilitation of the above diseases. Therefore, it is important to establish a simple, highly sensitive, recyclable and quick response method for the detection pH value of water.

Up to date, there are many approaches for the detection of pH value, such as potentiometric [7], acid-base indicator titration [8], nuclear magnetic resonance [9], UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy [10], fluorescence spectroscopy [11–14] and so on. However, in the pH detection of aqueous media or intracellular environment, some traditional methods including potentiometric, acid-base indicator titration, nuclear magnetic resonance and UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy have many drawbacks such as high requirements for equipment, moderate sensitivity and selectivity, and require frequent calibration. Comparing with traditional methods, fluorescence-based modes have highly selective, low cost, sensitive and convenient operation in pH detection of aqueous media or intracellular environment [15–17]. Fluorescence methods maybe a most promising alternative for pH detection of aqueous media or intracellular environment.

As an alternative emitter, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted growing research interest due to their excellent properties such as size-dependent photoluminescence, broad excitation window, high aqueous solubility, chemical inertness and high PL fluorescence quantum yields [18–20]. These unique properties of QDs provide a series of promising applications in photocatalysis, drug delivery, sensors, bioimaging and optical limiting [20,21]. Recently, some QDs-based pH sensors have been reported. Wu and his co-worker reported N-doped GQDs with a high quantum yield of 36.5%, which have a pH-sensitive between 1.81 and 8.96 [22]. Xin Hai et al utilized folic acid encapsulated GQDs exhibited sensitive response to pH of 3–9 [23]. The research group of Hu Xu synthesized Sodium 4-mercaptophenolate capped CdSe/ZnS quantum dots exhibited a good liner relationship to pH of 3.0–5.2 [24]. Although these QDs have a certain response to pH, their response ranges are somewhat narrow, only neutral or acidic.

In this work, we synthesize Trp- functionalized GQDs via a thermal cracking method using CA as the carbon source and Trp as the modifier and nitrogen sources. The schematic diagram of synthesis of Trp-GQDs is shown in Scheme 1 . The as-prepared Trp-GQDs has relatively narrow size distribution and shows strong blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation. More importantly, Trp-GQDs shows a good linear relationship between photoluminescence intensity and solution pH values in the range of 4–12. In addition, this GQDs also has a good response to pH values in river water and tap water.

Citric acid was purchased from Tianjin Hengshan Chemical Corporation. L-Tryptophan was obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Corporation. Sodium hydroxide was supplied by Tianjin Bot Chemical Corporation. Phosphate and Glacial acetic acid were supplied by Beijing Tianyukanghong Chemical Corporation. Boric acid was obtained from Tianjin Kewei Chemical Corporation. All the reagents were analytical purity and were used as received.

Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded on a Bruker Ten-sor27 spectrometer (Germany) with a KBr disc in the wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1. The UV–vis spectrum was recorded at room temperatureon a Phenix UV1900 in the wavelength range of 260–600 nm. X-Ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on the D8 Discover (Bruker AXS Ltd) under ambient conditions using a Cu Kα X-ray. TEM images were obtained from JEM 2100 F (Japan, JEOL) operating at 200 kV accelerating voltage. Fluorescence spectra was obtained by a Hitachi F-4600 spectrometer. Particle size distribution of Trp-GQDs were measured by ZS90 Nano Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement were obtained with a PHI 5700 ESCA spectrometer utilizing Al HR radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detail and description of experiment

ce:section.ce:para: The BR-buffer solution was prepared depending upon the literature. The mixture of 100 mL phosphoric acid, glacial acetic acid and boric acid tricarboxylic acid (0.04 mol/L) was prepared. Then, different volumes of sodium hydroxide (concentration 0.2 mol/L) were added to the above solution obtaining a series of BR-buffer solutions with a specific pH (4–12).

The Trp-GQDs were prepared directly by a thermal cracking method. Citric acid (1 g) and L-Tryptophan (0.05 g) were placed in 25 mL flask. After heating at 200 °C in heating jacket for 10 min, the mixture became uniform pale yellow gel. Before the mixture is completely carbonized, 2 mL of distilled water was added under heating. After repeat this operation (2 mL of distilled water was added before the mixture is completely carbonized) 10 times, the color of mixture turns from pale yellow to orange indicating the formation of Trp-GQDs. The mixture was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in 100 mL NaOH (1 mol/L). And the pH of mixed solution was adjusted to 7.0. Finally, the mixed solution was purified by dialysis (Mw = 500–1000 Da) against DI water for 24 h to remove unreacted raw materials. The Trp-GQDs dispersion was obtained to carry out the next experiment.The solid products were obtained by vacuum drying for structural characterization of Trp-GQDs.
In order to compare, the preparation of other Trp-GQDs is in the same way in addition to the addition different amounts of Trp.

To examine the pH response of Trp-GQDs, the same volumes of Trp-GQDs was dissolved in different pH values of BR-buffer solution. All solutions were excited at 360 nm with excitation wavelength band passes set as 2.5 nm.

For real water samples pH determination, river water (Tianjin Haihe River) was firstly filtrated through membrane (0.2 μm) and tap water is directly tested. In order to evaluate the detection performance of Trp-GQDs, an electronic pH meter (PHS-3C, Rex Electric Chemical) was used to determine the actual pH value of river and tap water.

In this work, the synthesis of Trp-GQDs according to the previous method described above. Schematic diagrams of the formation mechanism of Trp-GQDs is shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary material). During the formation of Trp-GQDs, the functionalization of GQDs also occurs simultaneously. In this process, CA acts as a carbon source and plays a crucial role in the formation of GQDs through dehydration and carbonization [25]. Trp is mainly used as nitrogen sources and functionalizing agent to improve the optical and chemical properties of GQDs. Functionalized functional groups can be introduced into GQDs in situ by covalent interactions between CA and Trp [26]. Be compare to the previous two-step method of synthesizing GQDs, this one-step method making Trp-GQDs exhibit more stable. This method shows a greater advantage to design novel and high performance functionalized GQDs.

Moderate amount of functional reagents are essential for surface functional groups and the fluorescent properties of functionalized GQDs [27]. A small amount of tryptophan makes the structure of GQDs relatively complete, but the functionalization is incomplete and the functional groups are less. However, excess tryptophan may consume too much citric acid, resulting in smaller GQDs, even failure to form GQDs, disrupting the integrity of GQDs, and to some extent causing fluorescence quenching of GQDs. In order to find a suitable amount of tryptophan, Trp-GQDs of different degrees of functionalization were prepared by adjusting the amount of Trp while fixing 1 g of CA. The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Trp amount is given in Fig. S2. All Trp functionalized GQDs exhibit stronger fluorescence intensity than simple GQDs, indicating that the addition of Trp can significantly increase the fluorescence intensity. The amount of Trp increasing from 0 to 0.05 g, the fluorescence intensity of the Trp-GQDs was gradually increased at the emission peak 457 nm. The fluorescence intensity reached a maximum when Trp reached 0.05 g. However, continued increase in the amount of tryptophan results in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity, which may be because excess tryptophan may disturb the polymerization of citric acid and reduce the conjugation of the product, resulting in fluorescence quenching. To obtain optimal the fluorescence intensity, 0.05 g Trp was used in the following experiments to synthesize Trp-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characterization of Trp-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared Trp-GQDs were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the prepared Trp-GQDs have a diameter of about 7 nm according to their particle size distribution. In addition, a high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1B) shows a lattice plane corresponding to a (100) plane of graphite carbon with a pitch of 0.33 nm [28]. In the Fig. 2 C, the XRD pattern of Trp-GQDs exhibit a broad peak centered at 23.9° (0.35 nm), which can be directed to the (002) crystal plane of graphite. This demonstrates that the melting pyrolysis of tryptophan and citric acid forms the structure of GQDs.

FT-IR was utilized to analyse the chemical structure of Trp-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2, FT-IR spectroscopy of Trp-GQDs showed an absorption peak around 2922 cm−1, which is a typical CH stretching vibration characteristic absorption peak, confirming that CA is not completely carbonized. The absorption at 434 cm−1 is OH stretching vibration and amine-based NH stretching vibration. The characteristic peaks at 1602, 1421, 1125 cm−1 are attributed to CO stretching vibration, CN stretching vibration, and CO stretching vibration, respectively. In particular, the absorption peak at 1350 cm−1 corresponds to CN stretching vibration of indole ring [29], which indicate the existence of Trp group.

To further analyse the structure of Trp-GQDs, XPS analysis was performed. In the Fig. 3 A, the broad spectrum of the XPS scan of Trp-GQDs shows the presence of C (C1s, 286.9 eV), N (N1 s, 400.9 eV) and O (O1 s, 533.1 eV). The XPS of the high-resolution C1 s (Fig. 3B) shows that the four peaks associated with CC/CC, CN, CO, CO are placed at 284.1, 286.0, 287.5 and 288.5 eV, respectively [30]. As shown in Fig. 3C, there are two peaks at 399.2 and 402.5 eV in the N1 s spectrum, indicating the presence of graphitic nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen [31]. The two peaks at 532.3 eV and 533.9 eV in the O1 s spectrum (Fig. 3D) are from the CO and CO groups, respectively [30]. Therefore, the surface or edge of Trp-GQDs carries abundant oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups. The existence of these hydrophilic functional groups endows the Trp-GQDs with high water solubility.

The optical properties of the prepared Trp-GQDs are presented in Fig. 4 . In the Fig. 4A, the prepared Trp-GQDs have a distinct UV–vis absorption band at 360 nm due to the n-π* transition of CO [32]. The absorption band at 278 nm is attribute to the typical absorption of an aromatic π system, which indicate the existence of aromatic of Trp-GQDs [26]. And this characteristic absorption corresponds to the absorption peak of Trp, which further proves the functionalization of Trp. The prepared aqueous solution of Trp-GQDs is yellow and transparent in daylight, and the Trp-GQDs solution produces bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 4B, GQDs and Trp-GQDs exhibit characteristic emission peaks at 486 and 459 nm at 395 and 375 nm excitation, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of Trp-GQDs is 7 times higher than that of synthetic GQDs. The fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength blue shift of Trp-GQDs and the above fluorescence intensity enhancement should be derived from the different electronic structures and sizes of Trp-GQDs and GQDs.

The Trp with an indole group which can change the surface of the GQDs and make the electron transfer from the pyrrole-N atom to surface of GQDs, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S3, we changed the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 400 nm, and the emission peak position did not change significantly. The position of the emission wavelength is always centered at 450 nm and the fluorescence intensity is the strongest at 360 nm excitation. This excitation-spectrum-dependent fluorescence emission spectrum indicates that the size and surface state of the Trp-GQDs are relatively uniform [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL response of Trp-GQDs for pH
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. S4, as the pH of the solution increased, the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the Trp-GQDs solution under 365 nm UV light was observed. It can be seen from the fluorescence spectrum of Fig. 5 A that the maximum fluorescence emission (450 nm) of Trp-GQDs at 360 nm is decreased as the pH increases from 4.0 to 12.0. Fluorescence emission of Trp-GQDs is very sensitive to changes in pH due to the presence of abundant functional groups such as amines, hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and pyrrole rings. It is worth mentioning that there is a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH in the range of pH 4.0∼12.0, as shown in Fig. 5B. The linear regression equation (The error bar is shown in Fig. S4) is
(1) F = −100.0 pH + 1779 (R2 = 0.98)

According to the literature, the nitrogen atom in the pyrrole can accept protons. The decrease in pH leads to the protonation of the pyrrole nitrogen atom of Trp-GQDs, and the proton transfer from protonated nitrogen to the conjugated carbon structure enhances the fluorescence intensity of Trp-GQDs. In addition, protonation and the resulting strong hydrogen bonds can have a greater contribution in the excited state than in the ground state, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity [35]. This observation is consistent with previous reports [36]. On the other hand, the pH response of Trp-GQDs may be due in part to changes in the concentration of H+ and OH−, which leads to electronic transitions of π-π* and n-π* in Trp-GQDs by refilling or consuming their Valence band.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential analysis of Trp-GQDs at different pH
ce:section.ce:para: To make clear the reason for pH-dependent PLemission, the zeta potential of Trp-GQDs solution at pH 4–12 was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , as the pH increases from 4 to 12, the zeta potential changes from −11.94 mV to −36.18 mV. The linear relationship between Zeta potential (ζ) and pH (4.0–12.0) can be expressed as ζ = −3.03 pH + 0.18 (R2 = 0.99) (The error bar is shown in Fig. S5). This indicates that functional groups such as amino, carboxyl and pyrrole rings on the surface of Trp-GQDs can undergo reversible protonation. This is similar to the nature of amino acids [22]. The electronegativity of Trp-GQDs increases with increasing pH. Conversely, the smaller the pH, the more positive Trp-GQDs are.

To demonstrate that the fluorescence of Trp-GQDs has a reversible change between acidic and basic, the pH of the Trp-GQDs solution was adjusted from 4.0 to 12.0 and then adjusted back to 4.0 for 7 consecutive cycles. Fig. 7 shows that the fluorescence intensity of Trp-GQDs showed little change after repeated adjustments of pH for 7 cycles with a relative standard deviation of 2.2%. Further proof of the protonation-deprotonation process of the fluorescence of Trp-GQDs in an acid-alkaline environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH detection of environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate the ability to probe solution pH, the PL response of Trp-GOSs under the existence of common cations was also investigated (Fig. S6, these common cations don’t affect the fluorescence intensity of Trp-GOSs, this indicate this probe have a high selectivity to proton. The pH-sensitive properties of the fluorescent emission of Trp-GQDs can be applied to the pH detection of water bodies to ascertain whether the water is contaminated or safe to drink. As shown in Table 1 , the relative standard deviation of the pH values of the two waters measured by our method relative to the acidity meter is less than 5%. We can note that the results are reliable, so Trp-GQDs can be used as excellent fluorescence pH indicator for water samples.

In summary, we successfully utilized Trp and CA as precursors to synthesize Trp-GQDs by a thermal cracking method. Doping of Trp effectively improves the luminescent properties of GQDs. The prepared Trp-GQDs have an average diameter of 7 nm and the shape is relatively regular. As a dispersion probe, the Trp-GQDs exhibit pH-sensitive fluorescence characteristics and have a nice linear relationship in the range of 4–12. In addition, this pH nanosensor has good accuracy and repeatability in pH detection in real water samples.
There are no conflicts to declare

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Educational Committee of Hebei Province (No. ZD2017214).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2018.12.014.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
